title: Ad Hoc Access and Secure Collaboration
description: Grant ad hoc access to individual objects, troubleshoot access control issues, and implement security labels and agreements for secure collaboration.

topics.title: Ad Hoc Access Control

content.text: Policy rules created in the Policy Administrator provide default access for all objects of a specific type. Ad hoc access control lets you grant additional permissions for individual object instances above the policy-based rules.
Ad Hoc Capabilities and Limitations

rows: Only grant access (never deny)
Cannot override security labels
Provide temporary access via lifecycle templates
Cannot override absolute deny permissions
Available through Edit Access Control interface
Changes affect only the individual object, not the type
Context managers and users with Change Permissions can use
Granting user can only assign permissions they have or a subset

content.text: Domain policy rules apply to all objects of a specific type. Ad hoc rules apply to individual object instances. Changes to domain rules immediately affect all objects; changes to ad hoc rules affect only the specific object.
When to Use Ad Hoc Access

cards.back: Access granted as part of a business process. For example, granting temporary Read access to all reviewers when a document is in the Under Review state.
Access granted by business administrators, context managers, or object creators through the Edit Access Control interface on individual objects.
In project and program contexts, ad hoc access enables sharing specific objects with users outside the project team for collaboration purposes.

content.prompt: Arrange these access control methods from broadest scope to narrowest:
items: Domain policy rules (all objects of a type in a context)
Lifecycle ad hoc rules (objects in a specific lifecycle state)
Manual ad hoc rules (one specific object instance)

keyTakeaways: Ad hoc access can only grant permissions - it cannot deny access.
Lifecycle ad hoc rules provide temporary access tied to a specific business process state.
Ad hoc access supersedes domain policy deny rules but cannot override security labels or absolute deny.
Only users with Change Permissions access can grant ad hoc permissions to others.

topics.title: Troubleshooting Access Control Issues

content.text: When users report access problems, the Edit Access Control dialog and the View Access Information report provide the tools needed to diagnose and resolve issues.
Edit Access Control Dialog

Access the Edit Access Control dialog from the Actions menu of any object. This dialog shows participants with access and the current permissions granted. Grayed-out permissions cannot be changed through this interface.

rows: Team Access
Permissions for all context team roles
Defined Access
Permissions explicitly defined for the object
My Access
Your own permissions on the object

content.text: View Access Information Report

The View Access Information icon on the Edit Access Control page launches a consolidated view of all individual rules that produce the final permission set. This report shows whether each permission comes from a policy rule, lifecycle ad hoc rule, manual ad hoc rule, or security label denial.

The Access Information page cannot modify rules directly, but it shows the exact rule or combination of rules causing the access issue. Use this information to locate and modify the correct rule in the Policy Administrator.
Remember: if a participant is both granted and denied the same permission by different rules, the permission is NOT granted. If the participant has an absolute deny, no other rule can override it.

content.prompt: Match each troubleshooting scenario to the likely cause:

pairs.left: User can see the object but cannot download files
pairs.right: Has Read but lacks Download permission

pairs.left: User cannot see any objects in the product
pairs.right: Product is set to Private and user is not on the team

pairs.left: User has a grant rule but still cannot access
pairs.right: Absolute deny or security label is overriding the grant

pairs.left: User can modify some documents but not Outlines in review
pairs.right: State-specific deny rule on the object type

keyTakeaways: The Edit Access Control dialog shows current permissions and allows granting ad hoc access.
The View Access Information report shows the exact source of each permission for troubleshooting.
Grayed-out permissions in Edit Access Control cannot be changed through that interface.
Always check for absolute deny rules and security labels when a grant rule doesn't seem to work.

topics.title: Security Labels and Agreements

content.text: Security labels classify sensitive information and restrict access to only authorized users. They are the strongest rules in Windchill's access control scheme, overriding all domain and ad hoc rules.
How Security Labels Work
A security label placed on an object acts as a gate: if the user is not cleared for all security labels on the object, they cannot access it - regardless of any other access control rules.

rows: Label
One or more labels classifying the object
Corporate Proprietary
Label Values
Specific classification levels within a label
Public, Internal Personnel Only, Highly Trusted
Participants
Groups, users, or organizations authorized for each value
Internal Personnel Only → Employees group
Agreement
Workflow-driven exception to a security label
Non-disclosure agreement for one month

content.text: Predefined Security Label Examples

cards.back: Restricts objects based on standard export regulations. Objects using this label prompt users to acknowledge terms before downloading content. Authorized for a group named U.S. Persons.
Indicates corporate sensitivity for highly trusted internal personnel. The Private value is cleared for members of the Employees group.
A standard label that does not restrict access but indicates the object contains legal data. Used for informational classification only.
Indicates the object is subject to a third-party NDA. Applied and tracked through a custom Java class for automated enforcement.

content.text: Being an authorized participant for a security label does not grant access by itself. The user must still be granted access through domain or ad hoc rules. The security label only removes the denial - other rules determine the actual permissions.
Agreements

Agreements provide workflow-driven exceptions to security labels, enabling temporary access for users who would otherwise be denied. Agreements are lifecycle-controlled objects - if expired, inactive, or removed, the user's access is immediately revoked.

content.prompt: Arrange the steps for accessing a security-labeled object:
items: User must be cleared for ALL security labels on the object
If not cleared, user may gain access through a valid agreement
Domain or ad hoc rules determine the actual permissions granted
User can access the object with the permissions defined by rules

keyTakeaways: Security labels are the strongest access control rule, overriding all domain and ad hoc rules.
Users must be cleared for ALL labels on an object - a single uncleared label blocks all access.
Being an authorized participant only removes the security label denial - other rules define actual permissions.
Agreements provide temporary exceptions to security labels and are lifecycle-controlled objects.

topics.title: Grant Ad Hoc Access

content.text: In this exercise, you grant ad hoc access to a user who needs to work on a document in a restricted folder. Olive Watkins has Guest access but needs Modify permissions for a specific document.
You should be signed in as bpatterson/ptc (business administrator). You will also verify access as owatkins/ptc.

content.title: Grant Ad Hoc Access
content.objective: Grant Olive Watkins (owatkins) ad hoc Read and Modify access to a specific document in the Team Information folder.

tasks.title: Grant Olive Watkins Ad Hoc Access

steps.action: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Folders.
steps.detail: You should still be signed in to Windchill as bpatterson/ptc.
This folder has restricted access due to the folder domain you created in the previous exercise.

steps.action: Select the checkbox for the Specification 1 document.
steps.detail: You need to select the document before accessing its actions.

steps.action: From the Actions menu, select Edit Access Control.
steps.detail: The Edit Access Control dialog shows current participants and their permissions for this specific object.

steps.action: Add Olive Watkins (owatkins) to the Access table.
steps.detail: You can search for the user by name or username to add them as a participant.

steps.action: Select the Read and Modify permissions for Olive Watkins.
steps.detail: Guest roles have Read access by default, but the folder domain rule restricts Read and Download access to participants outside the context team. Ad hoc access overrides the domain deny rule.
The ad hoc permissions are saved. Olive Watkins now has Read and Modify access to the Specification 1 document.
You have granted ad hoc access to the document.
You need a fresh session to sign in as Olive Watkins.
Olive Watkins is a Guest user who was previously denied access to the Team Information folder.
This searches for objects with names starting with 'Specificat' to locate the document.

steps.action: Right-click the Specification 1 document and notice that Olive can now check out and edit the object.
steps.detail: The Check Out and Download action is not available because this document has been created with the No Content option. If the document contains content, the Modify Content permission grants the user the right to modify the primary content and attachments.
steps.hint: Notice that Check Out and edit actions are now available in the context menu.

keyTakeaways: Ad hoc access is granted through the Edit Access Control dialog on individual objects.
Ad hoc grants override domain-level deny rules (but not absolute deny or security labels).
Guest roles may need explicit ad hoc grants to access objects in restricted folders.
Always verify access changes by signing in as the affected user.
